metadata.summary: Three-stage pipeline: Haiku ingests behavioral data; Opus identifies clusters with silhouette validation; Sonnet generates persona narratives, JTBD maps, pain points.

content.title: AI-Assisted Persona Building
content.summary: A three-stage pipeline for building data-driven personas from behavioral analytics data: Haiku ingests and normalizes event exports; Opus identifies cluster boundaries with silhouette validation; Sonnet generates persona narratives, JTBD maps, and pain-point summaries. A mandatory human gate sits between clustering and narrative generation — the researcher names and validates clusters before the agent writes about them.

why.p: Assumption-based personas mislead roadmap decisions. AI clustering surfaces behavioral segments that human intuition misses; JTBD integration replaces vague demographic labels with actionable motivation statements. Cluster stability validation (silhouette score, multi-seed runs) prevents the most common failure: confident personas built on statistically weak segments.

list.item: Building initial personas from existing behavioral data (analytics, CRM, interview transcripts).
Updating stale personas when new behavioral data or cohort segments emerge.
Generating JTBD statements from clustered behavioral patterns.
Automating persona refresh as part of a recurring monthly or quarterly pipeline.
When no actual user data exists — without data the result is a synthetic persona, not a data-driven one.
As a final deliverable without team validation — AI clusters need human labeling and context.
When the user base is too homogeneous — clustering adds overhead without insight gain.
High-stakes segmentation (pricing tiers, feature gating) without statistical validation of cluster stability.

section.name: method
section.p: Data-driven persona building process with AI clustering, JTBD integration, and limitations of behavioral-data-only approaches.

rule.statement: Build personas from actual user data (analytics, interviews, surveys), not from assumptions; without data the result is a synthetic persona, not a data-driven one.
rule.rationale: Assumption-based personas tell engineers what they already assume. Data-derived segments surface behavioral patterns that intuition misses and that are defensible to stakeholders.

list.item: 1. Aggregate user data (analytics, CRM, interview transcripts, survey responses)
2. AI clustering of behavioral patterns to identify segment boundaries
3. AI-generated persona drafts from validated cluster centroids
4. Human validation and refinement — researcher names and labels each cluster
5. Ongoing persona updates from new data on a defined refresh cadence
Demographics + digital behavior (not just age and location)
Goals framed as JTBD: "When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [outcome]"
Pain points with quantified impact where data supports it
Behavior with trigger analysis: what event causes them to seek this product
Representative quotes backed by sentiment patterns from interview data

rule.statement: Use descriptive behavioral labels for clusters ("Power users who export weekly"), not demographic labels ("35-45 year old managers").
rule.rationale: Demographic labels introduce bias and tell designers what they already assumed. Behavioral labels are actionable for feature design and copy.

rule.statement: Include a persona retirement date in the document; personas without expiry become organizational mythology within 6-12 months.
rule.rationale: User behavior drifts with product changes, market shifts, and cohort turnover. Undated personas cannot be audited for staleness and mislead roadmap decisions.

rule.statement: Always combine behavioral data with qualitative data when generating JTBD statements; behavioral-data-only JTBD produces feature descriptions, not motivations.
rule.rationale: Analytics capture what users do, not why. JTBD inference from behavioral data alone produces statements like "wants to export data" when the underlying motivation is "needs to share progress with their manager to justify the tool spend."

section.name: limitations
section.p: Key limitations of AI-assisted persona building:

list.item: K-means requires a predefined k; agents choosing k arbitrarily produce misleading persona counts
Behavioral data from analytics tools (Mixpanel, Amplitude) captures what users do, not why — JTBD inference from behavioral data alone is unreliable
Personas built primarily from digital behavior underrepresent users who are active offline or on channels the product does not track
AI-generated persona narratives tend toward archetypes, not individuals — the "busy startup founder" persona tells engineers what they already assume
Persona drift: if data is not refreshed, personas become outdated and mislead roadmap decisions within 6-12 months

section.name: pipeline-stages
section.p: Three-stage Haiku/Opus/Sonnet pipeline with silhouette validation and mandatory human gate between clustering and narrative generation.

rule.statement: Run silhouette scoring or the elbow method before fixing k; never accept an agent's arbitrary cluster count choice.
rule.rationale: Agents given free choice of k will pick the number that makes the narrative cleanest, not the number statistically justified by the data. Silhouette score over 0.5 indicates strong clusters; under 0.25 indicates the feature selection needs revision.

rule.statement: Validate cluster stability by running clustering 3 times with different random seeds; if membership changes significantly, the segments are not robust.
rule.rationale: Unstable clusters produce personas that change with each run. Stability is a necessary condition for using personas to drive decisions.

rule.statement: Human gate between cluster identification (Opus) and narrative generation (Sonnet) is mandatory; the researcher names and validates clusters before Sonnet writes about them.
rule.rationale: A bad cluster produces a confidently-written persona that gets adopted without scrutiny. The human gate catches cluster misinterpretations before they become organizational mythology.

rule.statement: Tag each persona attribute as data-derived or assumed; downstream agents must not treat assumed attributes as facts.
rule.rationale: LLMs produce persona narratives that sound authoritative regardless of data quality. Explicit tagging forces honest calibration and makes personas auditable.

rule.statement: Agents generating JTBD statements from behavioral data without qualitative input will fabricate motivations; require citation of a data point or an explicit "inferred" flag.
rule.rationale: Behavioral data tells what, not why. JTBD statements require motivation — which is absent from analytics exports and must come from interviews.

rule.statement: Present personas in a team workshop before use; the act of naming and discussing clusters surfaces assumptions that the data cannot validate.
rule.rationale: Personas are organizational coordination tools. Team discussion reveals when a cluster label is misleading or when two clusters represent the same user type under different names.

template.purpose: K-means clustering on behavioral CSV with cluster summary output
Silhouette scoring to find optimal k; flags weak clusters
Prompt for generating structured persona JSON from validated clusters

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Best practices for AI-assisted persona building:

list.item: Always combine behavioral data (what users do) with qualitative data (why they do it); behavioral-only personas miss motivation and are not actionable for copy or feature design
Validate cluster stability by running clustering 3 times with different random seeds — if cluster membership changes significantly, the segments are not robust
Use descriptive behavioral labels for clusters ("Power users who export weekly") not demographic labels ("35-45 year old managers") — demographic labels introduce bias
Present personas in a team workshop before use; the act of naming and discussing personas surfaces assumptions that the data cannot validate
Include a "persona retirement date" in the document — personas without expiry become organizational mythology

section.name: ai-agent-pitfalls
section.p: Critical pitfalls when agents generate personas:

list.item: Agents generating JTBD statements from behavioral data alone will fabricate motivations; always require the agent to cite a data point or flag the statement as inferred
Cluster count (k) is a human decision, not an agent decision — agents given free choice will pick the number that makes the narrative cleanest, not the number statistically justified by the data
LLMs produce persona narratives that sound authoritative regardless of data quality; weak input data produces confident-sounding but unreliable personas
Human checkpoint mandatory between cluster identification and narrative generation — the researcher labels clusters before the agent writes about them
Persona documents generated by agents should be version-controlled and tagged with the data snapshot date; undated personas cannot be audited for staleness
